title: Spinning silk caps
subtitle: Preparing and spinning silk caps from degummed cocoon layers into a lustrous fine singles
excerpt: Silk caps are domed layers of degummed silk fibre, each cap the shape of a small bowl, used to spin a continuous lustrous singles by drawing fibres off the cap's edge. The technique requires patience: the fibre resists drafting until it finds its rhythm, and the result is a very fine, extremely strong yarn with a characteristic silk shine.
sourceNotes: Silk cap preparation and spinning documented in contemporary British and American spinning guild teaching. The technique of spinning from degummed silk caps is a revival of traditional reeled-silk preparation methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: Domed layers of degummed silk cocoon fibre stretched over a small form. Each cap is peeled off, placed over the hands, and spun from the edge by drawing fibres out continuously.
The removal of sericin, the natural protein glue that holds a silk cocoon together. Degummed silk is soft and spinnable; ungummed silk is stiff and sticky.
A spinning technique in which the fibre is drawn back a long distance from the orifice before being allowed to twist in. Used for silk caps to control the very slippery silk fibres.

content.text: (also called mawata caps) requires a slow, careful
to manage the extreme slipperiness of

-processed silk fibres. The caps are placed over both hands and fibres are drawn from the edge of the dome in a continuous filament. It is one of the most demanding silk preparation methods and produces the finest, most lustrous silk singles.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: A small amount of water or moisture on the fingertips to improve grip

content.text: Take one silk cap and gently stretch it from the dome over both hands, thumbs on the inside of the dome. The cap will open into a large, flattened oval across your hands. If the layers tear, work more slowly; silk caps tear easily when pulled unevenly.

The fibres in a silk cap run in all directions across the dome. At the edge of the cap there is usually a slightly thinner zone where fibres can be drawn out individually or in a very fine bundle. Pinch this edge between thumb and forefinger and draw back slowly. If the fibres tear rather than draft, add a tiny amount of moisture to your fingertips and try again at a different point on the edge.
Step 3: Spin with the wheel or spindle'

Attach the drawn fibre to the leader thread and begin spinning with a high twist ratio (14:1 or higher). Silk needs more twist than wool to hold together because it lacks the wool's scale surface. Treadle steadily and draw back smoothly: the silk will spiral into a fine, bright singles with very little crimp.

Ply two silk singles with S-twist to produce a 2-ply that is more stable for knitting or weaving. Set the twist by soaking in warm water, snapping gently between your hands, and hanging to dry. Silk does not need a weight to set the twist; the fibre's weight is sufficient.

items.problem: The silk fibres keep slipping off the cap instead of drawing out in a continuous strand
items.cause: Working from the cap face rather than the thinned edge, or dry fingertips that cannot grip the very fine fibres
items.fix: Work specifically from the very edge of the cap where the fibres are thinner and more aligned. Dampen the fingertips very slightly.

items.problem: The finished singles breaks easily when winding onto the niddy-noddy
items.cause: Under-twisting: not enough twist was inserted for the fine fibre to hold together under tension
items.fix: Increase the drive ratio and check the twist by cutting a short length and folding it back on itself. A correctly twisted silk singles should hold the fold without opening.
